one_line_takeaway: SentinelAgent formalizes multi-agent delegation-chain security as seven properties (six deterministic, one probabilistic intent-preservation property) enforced by a non-LLM Delegation Authority Service, showing on its own 516-scenario benchmark that combining scope-action and output-schema conformance closes the gap left by an intent classifier that degrades to 13% TPR under adversarial paraphrasing.
key_finding: On DelegationBench v4 (516 scenarios, 150 attacks / 366 benign), the ablation in Table VI shows: no defense 0% TPR / 0% FPR / 70.9% accuracy; P2 with off-the-shelf NLI 13.3% TPR / 0% FPR / 74.6% accuracy; P2 fine-tuned 88.3% TPR / 0% FPR / 96.6% accuracy; P6 alone 86.7% TPR / 0% FPR / 96.1% accuracy; P7 alone 13.3% TPR / 0% FPR / 74.6% accuracy; P6+P7 combined 100% TPR / 0% FPR / 100% accuracy; full P2+P6+P7 system 100% TPR / 0% FPR / 100% accuracy (95% Wilson CI: TPR [97.5%,100%], FPR [0.0%,1.0%]), with each of the 8 attack categories fully covered by at least one layer (Table VII). Fine-tuning the NLI model on 190 government delegation examples raises P2's standalone malicious TPR from 1.7% (off-the-shelf) to 88.3% +/-8.5% (5-fold CV, F1=82.1%), though isolated adversarial paraphrasing stress-testing drops P2 back to 13% TPR -- termed the 'adversarial intent paraphrasing problem' and flagged as the paper's central open limitation. Deterministic properties (P1,P3-P5) are reported unbreakable under all adversarial conditions tested (48/48). Black-box red team: 30/30 attacks blocked, 0/10 false positives. Independent red team: 45/45 blocked, 0/14 false positives. Combined across four independent adversarial evaluation methodologies (Table VIII): 100% TPR on 119 attack attempts, 0% FPR on 57 benign controls. End-to-end live LangChain/GPT-4o-mini integration: 5/5 attacks detected (100% TPR) via P6+P7, 0/23 benign steps falsely blocked. Measured latencies on the real prototype: token issuance 8.9ms, P6 scope check median 0.45ms (p95 0.66ms), P7 output validation median 0.45ms (p95 0.68ms), P4 forensic reconstruction 0.60ms (depth-2) / 0.03ms (depth-33), P5 cascade revocation 0.009ms; total per-step latency ~20ms dominated by NLI inference (~15ms). Composition safety: 45/50 concurrent chain scenarios safe without write-impact notification, 50/50 safe with it enabled.
core_contribution: SentinelAgent introduces the Delegation Chain Calculus (DCC), a formal model for multi-hop agent-to-agent delegation in federal multi-agent AI systems, defining seven properties over a delegation token tuple τ=(id,src,dst,σ,ι,π,hp,texp,sig): six deterministic (P1 authority monotonic narrowing, P3 policy conjunction preservation, P4 forensic reconstructibility in O(n), P5 bounded cascade containment, P6 scope-action conformance, P7 output schema conformance) and one probabilistic (P2 intent entailment preservation, via a three-layer keyword-filter + context-enriched-NLI + benign-override pipeline). The paper also proves four meta-theorems (property minimality via 7 concrete attacks; graceful degradation bounding damage envelopes to at most 1/11 of unconstrained actions per single-property evasion; defense-in-depth completeness over all 126 non-trivial evasion subsets of the 7 properties; composition safety for parallel chains, including a write-impact notification mechanism for chains sharing mutable state) and one proposition (practical infeasibility of deterministic intent verification, argued via a Rice's-theorem analogy plus 8 empirically constructed ambiguous delegation-intent pairs). It implements the Intent-Preserving Delegation Protocol (IPDP) enforced by a non-LLM Delegation Authority Service (DAS, ~350 LOC HTTP prototype with HMAC-SHA256 token signing), builds a Federal Compliance Mapping Engine (FCME) mapping 20 NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5.2 controls and 10 OWASP Agentic Security Initiative (ASI) risk categories to DCC properties, and constructs/releases DelegationBench v4, a 516-scenario benchmark (150 attacks across 8 categories A-H, 366 benign scenarios across categories E/I/J) for evaluating delegation-chain security. This is the third paper in a self-described series by the same author (after CivicShield for chatbot interfaces and RAGShield for RAG pipelines), addressing the multi-agent delegation layer.
threat_model: Seven adversary types (A1 External/prompt injection, A2 Compromised Agent, A3 Man-in-the-Middle, A4 Privilege Escalator, A5 Chain Poisoner/subtle intent drift, A6 Collusive Agents via side channel, A7 Protocol-Spanning) each mapped to documented real-world attack citations and to the DCC properties that defend against them (Table II). Adversary goals: execute unauthorized actions (G1), redirect agent behavior against intent (G2), bypass compliance (G3), evade forensic reconstruction (G4), maximize cascade blast radius (G5), execute unauthorized API calls within delegated scope (G6, the 'scope-action gap'), and produce malicious outputs from permitted API calls (G7, 'output manipulation'). Trust assumptions stated explicitly: the DAS itself is trusted and assumed not compromisable via prompt injection because it is deterministic software (not an LLM) that only evaluates set containment, cryptographic signatures, and NLI model outputs; the NLI/embedding models are pre-deployed with integrity-protected weights; network channels use TLS; at least one agent in any chain is honest. The paper is explicit that if the DAS itself is compromised (supply chain, insider, infra breach), all guarantees are void -- it proposes but only lightly evaluates mitigations (HSM key storage, 2-of-3 threshold signing, immutable audit logging, key rotation).
limitations: The paper's own 'Threats to Validity' section is unusually candid and should be preserved rather than softened: (1) Construct validity -- the 100% combined TPR is measured on a benchmark designed by the same author who designed the defense, and attack categories A-H were explicitly constructed to be catchable by P2, P6, or P7 respectively; mitigated only partially by the independent red team (45 attacks, different strategies) and the live LangChain evaluation (28 steps), not by an externally constructed adversarial benchmark. (2) P6 (scope-action conformance) cannot detect malicious use of a permitted API -- if an agent's granted scope includes query_records and it queries for unauthorized demographic profiling rather than eligibility verification, the call is within-manifest and P6 does not block it; this motivates but does not eliminate the P7 gap. (3) P7 (output schema conformance) validates only output *type tags*, not semantic content within a permitted type -- a permitted 'eligibility result' tag containing biased reasoning is not caught. (4) P2 intent verification degrades to 13% TPR under sophisticated adversarial paraphrasing when evaluated in isolation, and the theoretical justification for this being unfixable (Proposition 1) explicitly disclaims a formal reduction to Rice's theorem ('we do not claim a formal reduction... the gap between natural language and Turing machines precludes a direct application'), calling it only a structural analogy. (5) The DAS is a single point of trust; if compromised, all seven properties' guarantees are void. Fault tolerance is only simulated (2-of-3 threshold signing, 9 scenarios), not deployed against real Byzantine conditions. (6) The DCC does not model Privacy Act consent at all -- explicitly deferred to a future version. (7) Composition safety analysis only covers 50 scenarios, and the write-impact notification mechanism's overhead in high-throughput, many-concurrent-chain settings is flagged as unresolved future work. (8) Collusive agent side-channel attacks (A6) and implicit cross-chain influence propagation without formal delegation steps are explicitly named as open, unaddressed problems.

datasets_used: DelegationBench v4 (516 scenarios: 150 attacks across 8 categories A-H spanning 7 adversary types x 13 federal domains, 366 benign scenarios across categories E/I/J)
190 government delegation examples used for NLI fine-tuning (5-fold cross-validated)
8 hand-constructed semantically ambiguous delegation-intent pairs for Proposition 1's empirical demonstration
29 DAS robustness edge-case scenarios (path traversal, encoding attacks, replay, chain depth 32, etc.)
9 DAS fault-tolerance scenarios (2-of-3 threshold signing under crashed/Byzantine replicas)
quantitative_results: Full P2+P6+P7 system: 100% TPR at 0% FPR on 516 DelegationBench v4 scenarios (95% Wilson CI TPR [97.5%,100%], FPR [0.0%,1.0%])
P2 (off-the-shelf NLI) alone: 13.3% TPR / 0% FPR / 74.6% accuracy
P2 (fine-tuned NLI): 88.3% +/-8.5% TPR / F1=82.1% (5-fold CV) / 0% FPR / 96.6% accuracy
P6 alone: 86.7% TPR / 0% FPR / 96.1% accuracy
P7 alone: 13.3% TPR / 0% FPR / 74.6% accuracy
P6+P7 combined: 100% TPR / 0% FPR / 100% accuracy
No-defense baseline: 0% TPR / 0% FPR / 70.9% accuracy
P2 in isolation under adversarial paraphrasing stress test: 13% TPR (the 'adversarial intent paraphrasing problem')
P1,P3-P5 under adversarial stress: 48/48 = 100% unbroken
Black-box red team: 30/30 attacks blocked, 0/10 false positives
Independent red team (7 protocol-level strategies): 45/45 blocked, 0/14 false positives
Combined adversarial evaluation across 4 methodologies: 100% TPR on 119 attacks, 0% FPR on 57 benign controls
Live LangChain/GPT-4o-mini end-to-end: 5/5 attacks detected, 0/23 benign steps falsely blocked (28 total steps)
TLA+/TLC model checking: 2,744,789 states (largest config), 368,509 states (smaller config), zero invariant violations on P1,P3-P7
Meta-theorem coverage: all 126 non-trivial evasion combinations of the 7 properties enumerated
OWASP ASI mapping: full coverage on 9/10 risk categories (partial on ASI01 Goal Hijacking due to P2's probabilistic nature)
NIST 800-53 mapping: 20 controls across 9 control families
Composition safety: 45/50 scenarios safe without write-impact notification, 50/50 safe with it enabled
Fault tolerance: DAS survives 1 crashed replica (50/50 tokens verified) and 1 Byzantine replica (50/50 verified via 2 honest sigs); correctly refuses on 2 crashed (0/20) and Byzantine+crashed (0/20)

Measured latencies: token issuance 8.9ms; P6 median 0.45ms (p95 0.66ms); P7 median 0.45ms (p95 0.68ms); P4 reconstruction 0.60ms (depth-2), 0.03ms (depth-33); P5 revocation 0.009ms; total per-step ~20ms (NLI-dominated)
baselines_compared: SEAgent [15] -- mandatory access control, no delegation chains or intent preservation
ShieldAgent [16] -- probabilistic rule circuits for safety policy verification, no multi-agent delegation
Agent Behavioral Contracts [17] -- Design-by-Contract with probabilistic drift bounds, single-agent only

Agent Identity Protocol (AIP) [21] -- invocation-bound capability tokens with Biscuit/Datalog policies, 0.049ms verification in Rust; addresses identity/authority attenuation but not intent, compliance, or cascade containment
Authenticated Workflows [22] -- MAPL policy language, 100% recall on 174 test cases, engineering guarantees not formal proofs
FormalJudge [23] -- Z3 SMT solving for neuro-symbolic oversight, 16.6% improvement over LLM-as-a-Judge baseline; single-agent oversight only
ILION [24] -- deterministic execution gating at 143 microseconds mean latency, single-agent
Agentic JWT [25] -- dual-faceted intent token binding at authentication layer, no semantic intent verification
ASTRA [26] -- task-based access control with semantic scope matching, no intent-preservation-throughout-chain verification
Allegrini et al. [27] -- 31 temporal logic properties for single-host agentic AI safety, no multi-agent composition
South et al. [19] -- OAuth 2.0 extension for agent delegation authentication, no intent or compliance modeling
GroupGuard [30] -- collusive attack defense, addresses A6 threat vector that SentinelAgent explicitly defers to future work
PrivacyPAD [31] -- RL-based privacy-aware delegation without formal security properties
